sanskrit_devanagari:
एषा ब्राह्मी स्थितिः पार्थ नैनां प्राप्य विमुह्यति ।
स्थित्वास्यामन्तकालेऽपि ब्रह्मनिर्वाणमृच्छति ॥ ७२ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
eṣā brāhmī sthitiḥ pārtha naināṁ prāpya vimuhyati |
sthitvāsyām anta-kāle 'pi brahma-nirvāṇam ṛcchati || 72 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (canonical body text; sandhi sthitvā + asyām → sthitvāsyām, anta-kāle + api → anta-kāle 'pi)
exact (text body identical; punctuation differs — single danda)
exact (text body identical; verse-number marker rendered as ।।2.72।।)
exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_02.072 = MBh_06,024.072; verse closes the Sāṅkhya-Yoga chapter)

anvaya.meaning: at the end-time / at the time of death
in time / at the time (in compound anta-kāle)

translations.verbatim_quote: That is the way of the spiritual and godly life, after attaining which a man is not bewildered. If one is thus situated even at the hour of death, one can enter into the kingdom of God.

O Parth, such is the state of an enlightened soul that having attained it, one is never again deluded. Being established in this consciousness even at the hour of death, one is liberated from the cycle of life and death and reaches the Supreme Abode of God.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: One can attain Kṛṣṇa consciousness or divine life at once, within a second — or one may not attain such a state of life even after millions of births. It is only a matter of understanding and accepting the fact. Khaṭvāṅga Mahārāja attained this state of life just a few minutes before his death, by surrendering unto Kṛṣṇa.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Prabhupada reads brāhmī-sthiti specifically as Kṛṣṇa consciousness — the Gaudiya Vaishnava equivalent of the unitive state. brahma-nirvāṇa is glossed as 'kingdom of God' / entry into the Supreme Abode. The verse closes the chapter by naming the destination: attaining this state, one is no longer bewildered (vimuhyati), and even attaining it at the hour of death (anta-kāle 'pi) yields the terminal liberation. Prabhupada specifically identifies this verse as the 'perfectional stage' that 2.71's four-fold disposition prepares for.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Esa, this — the state described in the foregoing verses; brahmi sthitih, is the state of being established in Brahman, the state which is described as steadfastness in jnana, knowledge — abandoning all desires etc; he, partha, son of Pritha; prapya, attaining; enam, this — the state of one who has the steady wisdom; na vimuhyati, does not become deluded — does not get into the trap of further accepting and rejecting things; sthitva, being established; asyam, in this state — even briefly, even at the close of life; api, even; antakale, at the end-time, the moment of death; rcchati, attains; brahma-nirvanam, identification with Brahman, Liberation, mokṣa.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara is precise: brāhmī sthiti is the state of being established in Brahman, identical with steadfastness in jñāna; brahma-nirvāṇa is glossed as identification with Brahman, mokṣa, Liberation. The structural force of the verse: this state, once attained, no longer admits further bewilderment (vimuhyati negated); even brief establishment in it at the moment of death suffices for terminal liberation. The verse explicitly closes the chapter — Shankara reads eṣā as referring back over the entire foregoing description (especially 2.55-2.71). The chapter has been arguing toward this state since 2.11; the closing verse names it.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: This, O Pārtha, is brāhmī sthiti — the state in which the aspirant abides in Brahman, that is, in the Self qualified by devotion to the Supreme Person. Having attained this state the aspirant is no longer bewildered. Established in it even at the moment of death, he attains brahma-nirvāṇa — the cessation of all bondage and the entry into the realm of the Supreme.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads brāhmī sthiti as the state of abiding in Brahman where Brahman is the Self qualified by devotion to the Supreme Person (Vishishtadvaita). brahma-nirvāṇa is glossed as cessation-of-bondage plus entry into the realm of the Supreme. The closing structural force is identical to Shankara's: this verse names the destination of the entire chapter; even momentary establishment at death suffices. The traditions diverge sharply on the metaphysics of the destination but agree precisely on the verse's chapter-closing function.

positions.rendering: the way of the spiritual and godly life (Gaudiya: Kṛṣṇa-consciousness)
the state of an enlightened soul
the state of being established in Brahman, identical with steadfastness in jñāna (Advaita: nondual)
the state of abiding in Brahman as Self qualified by devotion to the Supreme Person (Vishishtadvaita)

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: brāhmī sthiti is the most metaphysically loaded term in the chapter. The four traditional readings diverge sharply on the metaphysics: Advaita reads brāhmī as referring to nondual Brahman; Vishishtadvaita reads brāhmī as Brahman-qualified-by-devotion-to-the-Supreme; Gaudiya Vaishnava reads brāhmī sthiti as the equivalent of Kṛṣṇa-consciousness. Modern translations (Prabhupada, Mukundananda) tend to flatten or specify per their own tradition. The Sanskrit term itself is irreducible — any English rendering must choose a tradition. The source pack therefore preserves all three traditional renderings explicitly. For an engineering layer, this is a hard scope-mismatch: the metaphysics of brāhmī sthiti is not engineering-translatable, and any analog must stay at the operational layer and acknowledge the scope-mismatch honestly.

positions.rendering: the kingdom of God (Gaudiya: Vaikuṇṭha / supreme abode)
liberated from the cycle of life and death and reaches the Supreme Abode of God
identification with Brahman, Liberation, mokṣa (Advaita: nondual identification)
cessation of all bondage and entry into the realm of the Supreme (Vishishtadvaita)

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: brahma-nirvāṇa is also irreducibly traditional. Advaita reads it as Brahman-identification (nondual). Vishishtadvaita reads it as cessation-plus-entry. Gaudiya reads it as entry into the supreme Vaikuṇṭha abode. The compound is a Sanskrit technical term whose force comes from the conjoining of two heavyweight terms: brahma (Brahman, ultimate Reality) and nirvāṇa (extinction, also a major Buddhist term). Any English rendering must choose a frame; the source pack records all three. Engineering analog cannot translate this; honest scope-acknowledgement is required.

positions.rendering: even at the end-time, the moment of death; even briefly, even at the close of life suffices
established in it even at the moment of death
if one is thus situated even at the hour of death

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All three traditions agree on the surface meaning (even at the moment of death). They diverge on the implication: Shankara emphasises the sufficiency of brief establishment at death for terminal liberation. Ramanuja and Prabhupada read it as the qualifying condition for entry into the supreme abode. The Sanskrit api ('even') carries the structural force — the verse is saying that the state, once attained, remains stable through to death itself, and even momentary attainment at the moment of death yields the terminal result. This anti-deathbed-anxiety force is preserved across all three traditions.

literal_meaning: This, O son of Pṛthā, is the state in Brahman; having attained this, one is not deluded. Established in it even at the time of death, one attains brahma-nirvāṇa.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna closes the chapter with the verse that names its destination. eṣā ('this') refers back over the entire foregoing description — especially 2.55-2.71 — and names that state brāhmī sthiti, the state in / pertaining to Brahman. Once attained (prāpya), one is no longer bewildered (vimuhyati negated). Even brief establishment in this state at the moment of death (anta-kāle 'pi) yields brahma-nirvāṇa, the terminal liberation. The three commentarial traditions diverge sharply on the metaphysics of brāhmī sthiti and brahma-nirvāṇa: Advaita reads them nondually (mokṣa, identification with Brahman); Vishishtadvaita reads them as devotion-qualified abiding plus entry into the realm of the Supreme; Gaudiya Vaishnava reads them as Kṛṣṇa-consciousness plus entry into the supreme abode. All three traditions agree precisely on the verse's chapter-closing function: this is the destination Krishna has been arguing toward since 2.11, and it is metaphysically maximally loaded. The chapter is over.

verbatim_quote_sources: bhagavad-gita.us (Ramanuja Gita-bhasya, Adidevananda tr. — paraphrase from JS-rendered page)
remaining_gaps: Ramanuja verbatim — page renders commentary in JS; only paraphrase recovered.
Madhva's Dvaita commentary not pulled — would add a fourth named tradition for the chapter-closing verse, which is the verse most worth the additional citation.
